caveats: a: No defensible compensation-to-receipts denominator was provided, and agricultural wage data omit much owner and family labor.
a: Adoption statistics measure tools and acreage, not labor hours or AI substitution, and larger farms adopt much more rapidly.
rho: Yield and input improvements are not all labor substitution.
rho: Farm size, tenancy, equipment age, field variability, and operator skill produce wide implementation differences.
e: The frozen dataset explicitly provides no defensible LMM firm count, so this conditional share cannot establish target volume.
e: Farm sales, household income, and accounting profit do not map cleanly to normalized EBITDA.
s5: Producer age is not owner age or sale intent, and one farm can have several producers.
s5: Most agricultural transfers are not necessarily arm's-length control acquisitions of an eligible operating firm.
q: Marketing and pricing evidence covers corn and wheat as well as soybeans.
q: Weather and commodity-price movements can swamp an efficiency gain even when the gain itself is retained.
d5: The long-term baseline used here was completed before current 2026 market conditions and is not a forecast.
d5: Production bushels mix biological yield and acreage and do not isolate eligible LMM farms.
o: Operations may consolidate or shift to custom farming without eliminating the operator requirement.
o: Automation can sharply reduce on-farm labor even when an accountable operator remains.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring seasonal commodity sales by bushel through cash markets, cooperatives, forward or marketing contracts, futures-linked arrangements, and specialty or identity-preserved contracts, with prices shaped by national and global supply, demand, quality, basis, delivery, and storage.
lens.excluded_activities: Passive farmland ownership, cash-rent-only entities, hobby and subsistence farms, captive production, grain merchandising without farming, custom crop services classified elsewhere, non-control financing vehicles, and operations without transferable control.
lens.included_activities: Independent U.S. farm operating businesses repeatedly planting, managing, harvesting, storing, marketing, and selling soybeans to external elevators, processors, exporters, cooperatives, or other buyers, within the specified EBITDA band.

a.bridge: USDA ERS measures technology adoption rather than wage-weighted AI task exposure: automated guidance was used on well over half of soybean acreage, 68% of the largest soybean farms used guidance in 2018 versus 11% of the smallest, and yield mapping and variable-rate technologies are established. I infer remaining exposure in planning, scouting, input decisions, machinery optimization, marketing, records, and administration after subtracting already automated guidance. Farm-owner unpaid labor and highly physical seasonal work likely make employee-wage evidence overstate total operating-work exposure.
caveats: No defensible compensation-to-receipts denominator was provided, and agricultural wage data omit much owner and family labor.
Adoption statistics measure tools and acreage, not labor hours or AI substitution, and larger farms adopt much more rapidly.
a.change_evidence: A soybean-specific time-and-compensation study by farm size, linked to ARMS adoption data and separating owner, family, employee, and contractor tasks plus current automation, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: AI can assist weather and yield forecasting, field and pest monitoring, prescription generation, equipment maintenance, input purchasing, logistics, marketing, insurance, compliance, and bookkeeping. Planting, spraying, repairs, grain handling, harvest, safety decisions, and irregular field work remain physical and time-critical.

d5.bridge: USDA's 2024 long-term baseline projected soybean production from 4.520 billion bushels in 2025/26 to 4.780 billion in 2031/32, about a 6% increase, driven mainly by yield while planted area stayed flat. USDA's July 2026 outlook now forecasts a record 4.5 billion bushels in 2026/27. I use the baseline quantity path as a five-year ratio, widening for weather, trade, biofuel, crush, acreage, and policy uncertainty. National production can grow while individual farm acres or market share fall.
caveats: The long-term baseline used here was completed before current 2026 market conditions and is not a forecast.
Production bushels mix biological yield and acreage and do not isolate eligible LMM farms.
d5.change_evidence: The latest USDA long-term baseline through 2031/32, with soybean acreage, yield, crush, exports, and biofuel assumptions plus regional projections, would materially improve the estimate.
d5.rationale: Domestic crush, renewable fuel demand, feed, exports, yield improvement, and rotation with corn support volume. Brazilian competition, China trade exposure, weather, disease, acreage competition, policy change, and yield-driven consolidation create downside.

caveats: The frozen dataset explicitly provides no defensible LMM firm count, so this conditional share cannot establish target volume.
Farm sales, household income, and accounting profit do not map cleanly to normalized EBITDA.
e.change_evidence: A USDA ARMS or Census microdata tabulation of 111110 businesses in the EBITDA band, matched to legal entity, external sales, crop mix, land tenure, leases, normalized earnings, and transferability, would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: Conditional on actually being in the $1 million to $10 million normalized EBITDA band, many independent commercial soybean operations would have recurring external sales and transferable equipment, leases, relationships, and operating systems. Eligibility is reduced by mixed-crop primary classification, family or sole-proprietor structures, dependence on personally owned land, leased acreage that does not transfer, government-supported or volatile normalized earnings, and asset sales that do not preserve the firm.

l.detail: No receipts concept for this code (government / SUSB out of scope). QCEW government wages exist ($553,052) but there is no revenue denominator; labor_share left null.
l.rationale: dataset labor_share has no defensible value

n.detail: margin 0.0804 (margins.json[11]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Farming/Agriculture EBITDA/Sales 8.04%); revenue/employee $299,458 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 11); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Operations may consolidate or shift to custom farming without eliminating the operator requirement.
Automation can sharply reduce on-farm labor even when an accountable operator remains.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of widespread fully autonomous farming supplied directly by equipment or software vendors under their own agronomic and commercial accountability would lower the estimate.
o.rationale: Remaining soybean demand requires an accountable farm operator to control land access, choose and execute agronomy, manage equipment and contractors, comply with pesticide and conservation rules, bear weather and market risk, and deliver the crop. AI and autonomy can reduce labor but cannot replace the operating entity within five years.

q.bridge: USDA ERS reports that less than 20% of soybean, corn, and wheat production is under marketing contracts, while cash, futures, options, and contracts use market-discovered prices. I translate this commodity-pricing structure into benefit retention. Individual farms generally cannot influence the commodity price, so an internal efficiency gain is not contractually passed through, supporting initial retention; over five years, land rents, machinery and input suppliers, custom rates, basis, and competitive acreage bidding absorb part of it.
caveats: Marketing and pricing evidence covers corn and wheat as well as soybeans.
Weather and commodity-price movements can swamp an efficiency gain even when the gain itself is retained.
q.change_evidence: Eligible-farm grain contracts, basis and premium histories, cash rents, input prices, custom rates, and farm-level margin persistence after verified automation would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Most buyers pay market and basis rather than auditing farm costs. Specialty premiums and storage timing can protect value, but landlords, suppliers, lenders, and competing growers capture some durable productivity gains.

caveats: Yield and input improvements are not all labor substitution.
Farm size, tenancy, equipment age, field variability, and operator skill produce wide implementation differences.
rho.change_evidence: Independent soybean-farm deployments reporting implementation cost, override rates, input savings, avoided labor hours, forecast error, yield outcomes, downtime, and adoption persistence across seasons would narrow the estimate.
rho.rationale: Modern farms already have GPS equipment, yield data, input maps, telematics, and digital market information, supporting implementation. Weather and biological uncertainty, fragmented records, rural connectivity, equipment interoperability, thin staffing, seasonal failure costs, data ownership, vendor dependence, and lower adoption on smaller farms constrain deployment.

s5.bridge: The 2022 Census of Agriculture measures all U.S. producers, not eligible soybean firms: average producer age was 58.1, 38% were age 65 or older, and 95% of farms were family farms. I translate aging and family ownership into five-year succession pressure, then discount because succession often occurs within families, land may transfer separately, leasing can continue, and retirement does not necessarily create a qualifying control sale. Large soybean operators may have more formal succession than the all-farm population.
caveats: Producer age is not owner age or sale intent, and one farm can have several producers.
Most agricultural transfers are not necessarily arm's-length control acquisitions of an eligible operating firm.
s5.change_evidence: USDA TOTAL successor-designation data or a soybean-belt survey linked to farm size and legal entity, plus transaction records distinguishing operating-company sales from land and equipment transfers, would replace the proxy.
s5.rationale: Aging operators, scale consolidation, family succession, and neighbors seeking acreage support transfers, while tax planning, intra-family gifts, land retention, cash rental, and piecemeal equipment or acreage sales often prevent a qualifying firm control transaction.

principal_driver: Large commercial soybean farms already generate field, yield, equipment, and market data that can support implementable AI decision tools.
principal_weakness: Missing labor-share and LMM-count inputs, plus unpaid family labor and asset-level farm transfers, make the economic opportunity difficult to size.
